coredata.dc:title: Visible light assisted photocatalytic reduction of CO2 to ethane using CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite photocatalyst
coredata.dc:creator: Kulandaivalu, Tharani
Abdul Rashid, Suraya
Sabli, Nordin
Tan, Tong Ling
coredata.dc:description: CO2 reduction through photocatalysis is considered a promising way to mitigate the abundance of this greenhouse gas in the earth's atmosphere. In this work, blue-fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized via a facile top-down hydrothermal method using biochar as the carbon source. The as-synthesized CQDs were incorporated together with commercial copper (I) oxide (Cu2O) nanoparticles to form CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite. The CQDs, Cu2O and CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite were then applied for gas phase photocatalytic reduction of CO2. The experiments were performed under visible light irradiation in a self-designated photoreactor which was connected to an online Gas Chromatography (GC). High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) confirmed the uniform deposition of CQDs with size ranging from 2.5 to 6.0 nm on the surface of Cu2O nanoparticles. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy further revealed the presence of CQDs on the surface of Cu2O. The CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite photocatalyst showed a considerable improvement in the CO2 photoreduction with an enhancement of 54% compared to the pure Cu2O. In addition, the band alignment of CQDs/Cu2O, charge carriers transfer and separation as well as possible reaction pathways for CO2 photoreduction was proposed. Finally, the photostability test revealed the CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite was able to retain its photostability of up to ~90% under five cycles of photoreaction.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Copper (I) oxide
Photocatalyst
CO2 photoreduction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphological studies of CQDs, Cu2O and CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite
XRD and FTIR studies of CQDs, Cu2O and CQDs-Cu2O nanocomposite
XPS studies of CQDs, Cu2O and CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite
Optical studies of CQDs, Cu2O and CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite
Possible mechanism of CO2 photoreduction to C2H6 using CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite photocatalyst

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank Ministry of Higher Education (MOHE), Malaysia for funding this research work under Fundamental Research Grant Scheme ( FRGS/1/2015/TK10/UPM/02/6 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustrating the synthesis process of CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite.
Schematic diagram of CO2 photoreduction system set-up.
HRTEM images of (a), (b) CQDs (c), (d) pure Cu2O and (e), (f) CQDs-Cu2O nanocomposite.
(a) XRD spectra and (b) FTIR spectra of CQDs, pure Cu2O and CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite.

XPS spectra of CQDs (a) Full survey, (b) C1s spectra (c) O1s spectra and XPS spectra of CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite (d) Full survey (inset: Cu2p spectrum of Cu2O and CQDs/Cu2O) (e) C1s spectra, (f) O1s spectra.

(a) UV–vis absorption and PL spectra of CQDs (concentration of CQDs = 2.0 mg/ml); inset shows the photographs of CQDs dispersion under daylight (left) and UV light of wavelength 365 nm (right), (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of Cu2O and CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite, (c) Tauc's plot of CQDs (inset), Cu2O and CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite and (d) PL spectra of Cu2O and CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite under excitation wavelength of 400 nm.
(a) Formation of C2H6 for 6 h of reaction time over pure CQDs, Cu2O and CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite photocatalyst, (b) Time dependence volumetric ratio of O2/N2 using CQDs/Cu2O photocatalyst.
Mechanism of photocatalytic reduction of CO2 over CQDs/Cu2O photocatalyst.
Photostability tests for CO2 photoreduction over Cu2O and CQDs/Cu2O photocatalyst via five consecutive test cycles.
Cu2O‑carbonaceuos materials integrated photocatalysts used for CO2 photoreduction.

~0.5 times higher than pure Cu2O

head.ce:title: Visible light assisted photocatalytic reduction of CO2 to ethane using CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite photocatalyst

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Advanced Technology, Universiti Putra Malaysia, Selangor 43400, Malaysia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Universiti Putra Malaysia, Selangor 43400, Malaysia

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemical Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Universiti Putra Malaysia, Selangor 43400, Malaysia.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: CO2 reduction through photocatalysis is considered a promising way to mitigate the abundance of this greenhouse gas in the earth's atmosphere. In this work, blue-fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized via a facile top-down hydrothermal method using biochar as the carbon source. The as-synthesized CQDs were incorporated together with commercial copper (I) oxide (Cu2O) nanoparticles to form CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite. The CQDs, Cu2O and CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite were then applied for gas phase photocatalytic reduction of CO2. The experiments were performed under visible light irradiation in a self-designated photoreactor which was connected to an online Gas Chromatography (GC). High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) confirmed the uniform deposition of CQDs with size ranging from 2.5 to 6.0 nm on the surface of Cu2O nanoparticles. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy further revealed the presence of CQDs on the surface of Cu2O. The CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite photocatalyst showed a considerable improvement in the CO2 photoreduction with an enhancement of 54% compared to the pure Cu2O. In addition, the band alignment of CQDs/Cu2O, charge carriers transfer and separation as well as possible reaction pathways for CO2 photoreduction was proposed. Finally, the photostability test revealed the CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite was able to retain its photostability of up to ~90% under five cycles of photoreaction.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past century, the significant rise of carbon dioxide (CO2) emission is being considered a major threat to the environment as it contributes to global warming. Recent Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) reports stated that the global mean concentration of CO2 has reached 400 ppm, overriding the safe level, which should be below 350 ppm [1]. Hence, there is a desperate need to take necessary measures to mitigate the abundance of CO2 in the earth's atmosphere. Though existing robust technology of carbon capture and sequestration (CCS) is regarded the most viable CO2 mitigation strategy [2], its high cost and energy input has drawn the attention of many towards alternative techniques such as, photocatalytic conversion, photochemical conversion and photoelectrochemical conversion [3,4]. Among this, photocatalytic conversion of CO2 using semiconductors as photocatalysts is more advantageous as (1) it is driven by boundless solar energy, (2) can be carried out at room temperature and pressure and (3) the hydrocarbon products evolved can be utilized to meet current energy demands [5]. TiO2 is the most commonly used photocatalyst for its excellent photocatalytic activity and stability. However, it is only photosensitive under UV light due to the wide bandgap energy (Eg = 3.02 eV) [6]. Hence, attention is being focused in developing visible-light active photocatalysts [7].

Copper (I) oxide or cuprous oxide (Cu2O) is a p-type semiconductor with a direct band gap of ~2.20 eV [8]. It is a promising photocatalytic material due to its abundance in nature, low toxicity, and ability to harness visible light [9]. On the contrary, photo-corrosion and fast recombination of electron-hole pairs restrict Cu2O application in the field of photocatalysis [10]. To overcome the limitations of Cu2O, various attempts have been employed to enhance the efficiency of its photocatalytic activity including surface engineering to control the exposed facets [11], coupling with n-type semiconductors (e.g. TiO2 [12], ZnO [13], Fe2O3 [14] and TaON [15]) and binary system formation with noble metals (e.g. Au [16], Ag [17] and Cu [18]). Meanwhile, the evolution of carbonaceous materials such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene and C60 have been witnessed in the field of photocatalysis which open up prospects in designing photocatalysts with higher photostability [19,20]. This has inspired the incorporation of Cu2O with carbon-based materials to escalate its photocatalytic activity [21].

Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles or carbon quantum dots (CQDs) belong to the class of carbon nanomaterials, composed of predominant sp2 hybridized carbon arranged in quasi-spherical particles of size less than ~10 nm [22]. Their unique properties such as good absorbance of solar light, excellent photoinduced electron transfer ability, facile functionalization of chemical groups and tunable photoluminescence (PL) have surged the research interest of scientists in designing CQDs-based photocatalysts [23,24]. These nanomaterials have seen to be utilized as a sole-photocatalyst [25] as well as coupling with metal oxides such as TiO2 [26], ZnO [27], Fe2O3 [28], SiO2 [29] to boost up the practical applications of these semiconductors in photocatalysis. The good electron accepting property and large electron capacity enable the extraction of electrons from these semiconductors to alleviate the recombination of charge carriers [30].

In this study, we attempted to develop a visible light responsive photocatalyst for gas phase CO2 photoreduction by incorporating CQDs and Cu2O, forming CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite. To date, few trials have been conducted in designing Cu2O‑carbonaceous materials integrated photocatalyst systems for this reaction which is summarized in Table 1 .

As displayed in Table 1, various hydrocarbon fuels have been evolved using the Cu2O‑carboneceus materials integrated photocatalysts depending on their experimental conditions, however this study will be of particular interest for the production of ethane (C2H6) from the CO2 conversion which has not been reported in any Cu2O-based photocatalyst studies before. C2H6 is of great importance for various industries such as a petrochemical feedstock for ethylene production and for its tendency to accelerate fruit ripening. It is worth to note that C2H6 consumes higher electrons compared to other hydrocarbon products which can be represented by Eqs. (1)–(4) [35]. This higher electrons demanding CO2 conversion reaction adds more value for this photocatalyst system and is expected to open new opportunities for other photocatalytic reactions.

(4)

CO 2
+ 14
H +
+ 14
e −
→
C 2
H 6
+ 4
H 2
O
ΔE 0
= − 0.27
V

Herein, CQDs were prepared and deposited onto the surface of Cu2O nanoparticles by a simple wet impregnation method. The resulting CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite was tested for CO2 photoreduction in gas phase under visible-light illumination. The prepared photocatalyst demonstrated enhanced photoactivity compared to the pure Cu2O, implying the effective role of CQDs for efficient charge separation. Therefore, coupling of Cu2O with CQDs significantly enhances the stability of the photocatalyst against photocorrosion, improves a higher separation of photogenerated charge carriers, and increases the CO2 photoreduction efficiency. A mechanism of reaction at the CQDs-Cu2O interface is also proposed. Finally, photostability test was carried out on both Cu2O and CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite to investigate the photostability of the Cu2O with the addition of CQDs.

Biochar from empty fruit bunches (EFB) of oil palms obtained from Pakar Go Green Sdn Bhd, were used as precursors to prepare CQDs via a facile top-down hydrothermal method. Absolute ethanol (95%) was purchased from Merck, Darmstadt, Germany. Copper (I) oxide nanopowder (Cu2O) was obtained from US Research Nanomaterials, Inc. All chemicals were of analytical grade and were used as received without any further purification. Deionized water was used throughout the experiment.

A known amount of EFB biochar particles were added to a steel vessel followed by 50% ethanol solution in the mass to volume ratio of 1:100. The vessel was sonicated for 5 min to attain a homogenous suspension, which was then heated for 1 h at 250 °C. After that, the vessel was cooled in a water bath for at least 4 h. The cooled mixture was then centrifuged for 5 min at 4000 rpm. Solid-liquid phases were separated and the supernatant solution containing CQDs were collected.

In a typical process, 0.2 g of commercial Cu2O nanopowder was dispersed in 10 ml of deionized water with the aid of a sonicator. About 2 ml of solution containing CQDs (2 mg/ml) was transferred into the dispersed Cu2O solution. This mixture was magnetically stirred for 4 h at room temperature and aged overnight. The mixture was finally washed with ethanol and deionized water several times before oven-dried at 80 °C for 12 h to obtain the CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite. This corresponds to 2 wt% of CQDs in Cu2O. Fig. 1 shows the schematic synthesis of the CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite.

The photoactivity of the developed CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite was tested at ambient temperature in a continuous mode, low pressure CO2 photoreduction system. Halogen lamp was used as the visible-light source, the distance between the light source and the photoreactor was fixed at 10 cm. The as-prepared photocatalyst with a mass of 0.2 g was loaded into the glass disc and was placed in the middle of reactor. After that, nitrogen gas was purged (at 50 ml/min) inside the reactor to remove any trapped gas and impurities. Then, highly pure CO2 (99.99%) was bubbled through deionized water prior to the release of gas mixture into the photoreduction reactor. When the system was absorption-desorption balanced in order to saturate the catalyst with CO2 and H2O (approximately 30 min), the light was switched on and the CO2 photoreduction process was conducted at 5 ml/min using a calibrated gas flow meter. The entire CO2 photoreduction system was kept inside an enclosed blank box to prevent any interference of light source from surroundings. The product gases evolved during the CO2 photoreduction process were monitored periodically for every hour and the concentration of the product gases was analysed by means of an online gas chromatography (GC) (Agilent 7890A) equipped with thermal conductivity detector (TCD) and flame ionization detector (FID). Nitrogen gas was used as carrier gas for both the detectors. Firstly, the gaseous products produced from the reaction were flowed into the TCD detector that was connected to a Carboxen 1010 column to analyze the light gases such as H2, O2, CO, CO2 and CH3OH. Then, the extracted gases were flowed into the FID detector to separate the C1 (methane) - C3 light hydrocarbons. Both these detectors were designed to detect gases with concentration as low as ~10 ppm. The schematic representation of photocatalytic reaction system set-up for CO2 photoreduction is shown in the Fig. 2 .

The morphologies of the samples were characterized using High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM, Tecnai TF20 X-Twin FEI Company). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI Quantera II, Physical Electronics) was employed to study surface chemical compositions of the samples. The C 1s line of spurious carbon located at binding energy of 284.8 eV was used as the reference to correct the binding energies for the charge shift. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the dried products were obtained by Shimadzu 6000 with Cu-Kα radiation at scanning rate of 1.2° min−1 from 20° to 80° to determine the crystal structure. The surface functional groups of the samples were investigated using Fourier Transform Infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet 6700, Thermo Nicolet) in the range of 400 to 4000 cm−1, at a resolution of 4 cm−1. The powdered samples were dispersed in potassium bromide (KBr) pellets to obtain a better spectrum. UV–vis spectra (UV–vis) of both the powder and liquid samples were recorded on a Lambda 35, Perkin Elmer spectrophotometer. The photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra were obtained using LS 55, Perkin Elmer PL spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological studies of CQDs, Cu2O and CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 (a) displays a typical HRTEM micrograph of monodispersed CQDs with the size ranging from 2.5 to 6.0 nm. The SAED pattern of CQDs in the inset of Fig. 3 (a) shows few bright spots which makes a nearly complete hexagonal shape indicating the crystalline nature of CQDs [36]. The HRTEM observation in Fig. 3 (b) confirmed the crystallinity of CQDs with lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (100) planes of graphitic carbon (d100 = 0.213 nm) [37]. On the other hand, pure Cu2O particles with size range of 40–50 nm showed several crystalline domains which represented by dark area, surrounded by amorphous region, as presented in Fig. 3 (c). The SAED pattern with diffused rings shown in the inset of Fig. 3 (c) indicates the polycrystallinity of the Cu2O particles. These nanoparticles have a lattice fringe of 0.25 nm, shown in the inset of Fig. 3 (d) corresponding to the (111) crystal plane of cubic Cu2O [38]. Fig. 3 (e) presents the HRTEM image of CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite where CQDs can be seen to be successfully decorated on the surface of Cu2O. A higher magnification of this image represented by Fig. 3 (f) provides a clear picture on the distribution of the quasi-spherical particles representing CQDs on Cu2O. The SAED pattern of the composites, displayed in the inset of Fig. 3 (e) shows discontinuous diffraction rings, primarily due to the presence of CQDs in the composite. The size and lattice fringes of the particles that was obtained from the HRTEM images agrees well with that of CQDs. Meanwhile, the crystals surrounded by the CQDs particles have lattice fringes of 0.25 nm, matching the lattice plane of Cu2O from Fig. 3 (d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: XRD and FTIR studies of CQDs, Cu2O and CQDs-Cu2O nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 displays the XRD patterns of pure CQDs, Cu2O and CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite. XRD spectrum of CQDs presents four major diffraction peaks at 2Ɵ = 28.4°, 40.6°, 50.2° and 58.7° which represent the crystal planes (002), (100), (102) and (103) of graphitic carbon [39]. The XRD spectrum also shows some other minor peaks that are suspected to belong to some impurities that might have been encountered during the freeze-drying process for sample preparation. Meanwhile, the sharp diffraction peaks at 2Ɵ = 29.1°, 34.6°, 42.2°, 53.5°, 61.5° and 76.6° of pure Cu2O ascribed to the planes (110), (111), (200), (211), (220) and (222) respectively, indexed to the standard Cu2O pattern in the form of cuprite [40,41]. As for the CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite, the diffraction pattern exhibits the similar pattern as the pure Cu2O. Note that, there was no sign of Cu2+ formation observed that might have arised from the oxidation of Cu+ during the preparation of the composite. Similar observation was reported previously [42]. Furthermore, no peaks corresponding to CQDs were detected in the diffractogram. This strongly implies that the introduction of CQDs at the current loading does not affect the crystalline phase of Cu2O. This phenomenon is explained by the trace amount and high dispersion of CQDs in this composite, which is supported by previously reported literatures [43,44]. Furthermore, no additional peaks were detected in the composite, indicating a virtually pure composite with negligible impurities if any. Fig. 4 (b) depicts the FTIR spectra of CQDs, pure Cu2O and CQDs/Cu2O composite. FTIR spectrum of CQDs shows a broad OH peak observed at stretching vibrations of 3290 cm−1, CH peak at 2970 cm−1 (and OH bending vibration at 1633 cm−1), CH peak at 2970 cm−1, CC peak at 1380–1486 cm−1, CO peak at 1047 cm−1 and COC at 881 cm−1 [45]. Meanwhile, both Cu2O and CQDs/Cu2O demonstrated a sharp peak at 530 cm−1 which belongs to the stretching vibration of CuO in the pristine Cu2O and the peaks at 3417 cm−1 and 2360 cm−1 are contributed from the physical and/or chemical adsorption of the atmospheric moisture and CO2 respectively [46]. By comparing with the pure Cu2O, the nanocomposite exhibited few additional functionalities contributed from CQDs such as COC, CO, CC and a weak CH peak.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XPS studies of CQDs, Cu2O and CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 (a) presents the XPS full survey spectrum of the CQDs, where it shows the existence of C and O elements. Fig. 5 (b) shows the deconvoluted peaks of C1s suggesting the existence of carbon elements in four diverse states in CQDs; CC at 284.6 eV and attachment with oxygen functionalities such as CO at 285.3 eV, CO at 286.1 eV, and COOH at 287.8 eV [47,48]. The O1s deconvolution spectra of CQDs shown in Fig. 5 (c) is fitted with CO, CO and COOH peaks. The presence of oxygen-functionalised groups describes the hydrophilic property of CQDs. Fig. 5 (d) shows the full survey spectrum of the CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite, whereby the presence of Cu2p, O1s and C1s peaks supported the presence of both Cu2O and CQDs in the nanocomposite. The high resolution of Cu2p spectrum of the nanocomposite (inset of Fig. 5 (d)) presents two dominant peaks of Cu2p3/2 and Cu2p1/2 for Cu+ from Cu2O and a weak peak at ~942.1 eV assigned to Cu2+ species that originates from the surface oxidation of Cu2O at ambient conditions [49]. On analyzing the Cu2p spectrum of both Cu2O and CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite, there is a shift in the binding energy that can be observed which is attributed to the disturbance of local environment and electron density around the Cu element due to the redistribution of the electric charges after the composite formation. Most significantly, the positive shift in the binding energy of Cu2p for the nanocomposite indicates that the electrons are being transferred from the conduction band of Cu2O to the CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite. As the electrons transfer, the electron density of the composite increases, subsequently leadings to the increase of binding energy of Cu [50]. This result supports the proposed mechanism, which will be discussed later. From the C1s deconvoluted peaks of CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite in Fig. 5 (e), four peaks were observed with the main peak corresponding to the CC at 284.6 eV and the rest assigned to the CO, CO and COOH bonds. High resolution spectra of O1s in Fig. 5 (f) fitted into four peaks, CuO bond from Cu2O and CO, CO and COOH bonds contributed from CQDs, as displayed in O1s spectra of CQDs. These results were in good agreement with the obtained FTIR results and further supports the presence of CQDs in the composite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical studies of CQDs, Cu2O and CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of the photocatalysts were studied using UV–vis and PL spectroscopy. Fig. 6 (a) displays the absorbance and PL spectra of CQDs. A strong optical absorption peak in the UV region can be spotted in the spectrum of CQDs that extends into the visible region. Moreover, a distinct absorption band around 210 nm was observed which is consistent with the previously reported CQDs [51,52]. This behavior was due to the π → π* transition of the aromatic structure CC bonds for carbon core [53]. Furthermore, the CQDs demonstrated an excitation-dependent PL emission, similar to the previously reported CQDs [54,55]. The PL peaks red-shifted from 420 nm to 540 nm with the increase of the excitation wavelengths and the maximum emission intensity occurs under excitation wavelength of 325 nm. This excitation dependent PL behaviour is most likely due to the quantum confinement effect from the various size distribution of the CQDs [56]. Fig. 6 (b) presents the absorbance spectra of pure Cu2O and CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite where a slight red shift in the absorption edge of the nanocomposite can be observed with respect to Cu2O which is from 610 nm to 630 nm. A Kubelka-Munk transformation was created through Tauc's relation to find the optical bandgap energies of the photocatalysts [39,57]. The bandgap energies of CQDs, Cu2O and CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite were estimated to be 4.6 eV, 2.0 eV and 1.91 eV respectively, as depicted in Fig. 6 (c). The narrow bandgap of the nanocomposite compared to that of pristine Cu2O is in accordance to the red shift in the absorption edge to higher wavelength. The PL spectra of the pure Cu2O and CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite under the excitation wavelength of 400 nm are shown in Fig. 6 (d). With the incorporation of CQDs, the emission of bare Cu2O has been quenched remarkably as the composite exhibited lower PL emission than that of Cu2O. This phenomenon suggests that the CQDs provide a secondary pathway for the transfer of photogenerated electrons at the CQDs-Cu2O interphase. This decreases the probability of the photoexcited electrons from recombining with the holes, hence more electrons can be consumed for the photoreduction of surface-adsorbed CO2 species.

The photocatalytic activities of pure CQDs, Cu2O and CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite were investigated for gas phase photoreduction of CO2 under visible-light irradiation and is summarized in Fig. 7 (a). Since the CQDs were prepared in the liquid phase, the sample was freeze-dried to obtain its powder form prior to use for the reaction. The photocatalytic experiment was performed after ensuring the system was stable by purging with N2 gas for at least 3 h. When there was no peaks detected by the GC, the photocatalytic experiment was started. For the photocatalytic reaction using Cu2O and CQDs/Cu2O photocatalysts, C2H6 was the only hydrocarbon product detected during the entire reaction period. Pure CQDs barely showed any photoreduction ability. As presented in the figure, the CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite photocatalyst showed enhanced photocatalytic performance towards CO2 photoreduction than that of pure Cu2O and CQDs. In addition to that, both Cu2O and CQDs/Cu2O photocatalysts showed a similar trend in photoreduction activity whereby the maximum yield of C2H6 was achieved after 3 h of continuous light exposure and the formation started to cease gradually after that. This might be due to the utilization of catalyst active sites for the molecular adsorption-desorption process. For the accumulated C2H6 generation, the CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite photocatalyst attained 54% of improvement in the photoactivity with the total amount of C2H6 generated of about 101.83 μmol/gcatalyst for 6 h of continuous light illumination, comparing to the pure Cu2O photocatalyst with total amount of C2H6 produced about 66.73 μmol/gcatalyst for the same reaction time. The improved photocatalytic performance of CQDs/Cu2O photocatalyst describes the role played by CQDs to facilitate the charge separation at the CQDs-Cu2O interphase, thereby inhibiting the recombination of electron-hole pairs, which was supported by PL results. This electron transfer mediating property of CQDs was observed in previously reported CQDs/ZnO and CQDs/SiO2 photocatalyst systems [27,58].

Without the addition of any sacrificial hole scavenger, H2O was used as the electron donor in this photocatalytic system. Preliminary photocatalytic tests were performed in the presence of only CO2 without H2O. No considerable amount of product gases were detected by GC throughout the reaction period by using all three photocatalysts. The results emphasized the importance of H2O for the reaction to occur. The quantitative analysis of the products of H2O oxidation such as O2 is crucial for better understanding of the reaction mechanism of CO2 photoreduction with H2O. Therefore, the concentration of O2 evolved using CQDs/Cu2O photocatalyst for CO2 photoreduction was monitored. Since the background levels of N2 and O2, which were present at few hundred ppm in the reactor could not be excluded, a better indicator for O2 production from CO2 photoreduction would be recording the volumetric ratio of O2/N2 in the effluent gas as suggested in previous works [59,60]. As shown in Fig. 7 (b), the trend of volumetric ratio of O2/N2 as a function of time using both Cu2O and CQDs/Cu2O photocatalysts is almost similar as that of their respective C2H6 formation from CO2 photoreduction under visible light irradiation. This implies that O2 is being generated from the oxidation of H2O during CO2 photoreduction reaction. In addition, the C2H6:O2 product stoichiometry was in the range of 1:2.96 to 1:3.42 (24.68 to 31.88 μmol/gcatalyst) for pure Cu2O and 1:3.11 to 1:3.40 (45.94 to 52.19 μmol/gcatalyst) for CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite which is very close to the 1:3.5 stoichiometry considering CO2 photoreduction was the only catalytic process operating; CO2 photoreduction to C2H6 is a 14 e− process (2CO2 + 14H+ + 14e− → C2H6 + 4H2O), in contrast to the 4 e− process of water splitting (2H2O → O2 + 4H+ + 4e−) where for each C2H6 molecule formation, 3.5 molecules of O2 are being released from water splitting process. From this, one can conclude that both Cu2O and CQDs/Cu2O may act as an efficient photocatalyst for CO2 photoreduction. However, considering the ratio of O2/N2 by CQDs/Cu2O always larger than by Cu2O suggests that CQDs/Cu2O was more active than Cu2O for O2 production, probably due to the decoration of CQDs on Cu2O which enhance the adsorption of H2O on the catalyst surface [61]. In contrast, since CQDs showed very poor photoactivity to reduce CO2, the O2 released was very minimal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of CO2 photoreduction to C2H6 using CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: In general, there are two possible ways of CO2 reduction to C2H6, which is from hydrogenation of ethylene intermediate or dimerization of two CH3 adsorbates [62]. However, there was no trace of methane or ethylene detected using both bare Cu2O and the nanocomposite throughout the reaction period. It was postulated that the formation of ethylene is more favorable on the single crystal Cu surfaces rather than polycrystalline surfaces [63]. Based on the SAED pattern of the pure Cu2O in Fig. 3 (c) which reveals its polycrystalline nature, it is very likely that the formation of C2H6 is not favorable through the hydrogenation of ethylene intermediate, therefore the other possible pathway which is through the dimerization of CH3 adsorbates was proposed for this study. The possible reason of methane (CH4) not being detected by the GC throughout the reaction is probably due to the rapid transformation of the intermediates (CH3) to C2H6.

The possible reactions occurring on both Cu2O and CQDs/Cu2O photocatalysts for the photoreduction of CO2 to C2H6 are represented by Eqs. (1) to (8). The CO2 photoreduction reaction is initiated when Cu2O becomes photo-excited upon visible light absorption and generates electrons (e−) and holes (h+) at the conduction band (CB) and valance band (VB) respectively, represented by Eq. (1). Pure Cu2O is capable of catalysing the redox reactions of CO2 to C2H6 (E0 = −0.27 V) and H2O to O2 (E0 = +0.82 V) since the redox potentials lie in between the VB (EVB = −1.0 V) and CB (EVB = +1.1 V) of Cu2O [64]. Hence, in the reaction catalysed by Cu2O alone, the electrons at the CB would participate in the photoreduction of adsorbed CO2 species to CO2 − radicals, as shown in Eq. (2) [65,66]. Meanwhile, as represented by Eqs. (3) and (4), the holes (h+) at the valence band of Cu2O participate in the photo-oxidation of H2O, yielding hydroxyl radicals (OH) and H+ and further oxidizing with OH radicals to form O2 and H+ [34,67]. The H+ ions then undergo a reduction process by reacting with electrons forming H radicals, as represented by the Eq. (5). Both the H and CO2 − radicals react continuously in a series of reactions to form CH3 on the catalyst surface, and finally the dimerization of two CH3 species forms C2H6. These reactions are represented by Eqs. (6) to (8). However, due to high recombination rate of charge carriers of Cu2O, most of the photogenerated electrons tend to recombine with holes in the VB of Cu2O and only a portion of charges would participate in the photocatalytic reaction [68]. When CQDs are deposited on the surface of Cu2O, these photogenerated charge carriers are extracted by CQDs to drive the photoreduction reaction. Therefore, in the case of CQDs/Cu2O it is proposed that all the reactions represented by Eqs. (1) to (8) occur on the surface of Cu2O except for the photoreduction reaction shown in Eq. (2) which occurs on the surface of CQDs rather than on Cu2O. This electron mediating property of CQDs reduces the probability of electron-hole recombination process along the Cu2O network which is supported by the high PL quenching of the nanocomposites compared to the pure Cu2O.
(1)
Cu 2
O
→ hv
e −
+
h +
(2)
CO 2
+
e −
→ ˙
CO 2 −
(3)
H 2
O +
h +
→ ˙ OH +
H +
(4)
˙ OH +
H 2
O + 3
h +
→ 3
H +
+
O 2
(5)
H +
+
e −
→ ˙ H
(6)
˙
CO 2 −
+ ˙ H → ˙ C
O −
→
H +
˙ C +
OH −
(7)
˙ C + 3 ˙ H → ˙
CH 3
(8)
˙
CH 3
+ ˙
CH 3
→
C 2
H 6

Based on the proposed mechanism, CQDs deposited on the surface of Cu2O play two important roles during the photocatalytic process. Firstly, they act as an electrons collector and transporter, to thoroughly extract the photogenerated electrons from the Cu2O, thus reducing the probability of electron-hole recombination process. In this way, the charge carriers will have more time to serve as reaction centers for the chemical processes [69]. Secondly, CQDs have large electron capacity where the redundant electrons from the Cu2O can be shuttled freely along their conducting network [22]. This reduces the possibility of self-reduction of Cu2O to the enhancement in the photostability of the composite. Besides, there was no trace of molecular H2 detected by the GC throughout the reaction time. This suggests that both H radicals and H2 were rapidly utilized by CO2 molecules during the photocatalytic reaction. Similar observations were reported in previously published works [70,71]. The overall mechanism of reaction for CO2 photoreduction to C2H6 over CQDs/Cu2O is illustrated in Fig. 8 .

The photostability of Cu2O and CQDs/Cu2O photocatalyst was investigated under the repeated photocatalytic experiment. The photocatalysts after each cycle were allowed to regenerate at room temperature overnight before the following cycle was performed, by keeping other reaction conditions constant. As shown in Fig. 9 , the total C2H6 formation using pure Cu2O dropped significantly over five consecutive cycles of photoreaction which explains its poor photostability. On the other hand, CQDs/Cu2O was able to retain its photoactivity of up to ~90% for the all five cycles, demonstrating its high photostability. The slight reduction in the photocatalytic activity of this photocatalyst after each cycle is probably due to the loss of active sites by the accumulation of carbonaceous intermediates [72]. Overall, this test shows that with the addition of CQDs, CQDs/Cu2O is highly photostable and recyclable.

CQDs were prepared from EFB biochar of oil palms and were decorated onto pure Cu2O using a simple wet impregnation method. The CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite photocatalyst with good optical, physical and chemical properties was able to reduce CO2 to C2H6 under visible light irradiation. The improvement in the photocatalytic activity of the CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite photocatalyst of up to 54% compared to that of pristine Cu2O demonstrated that incorporation of CQDs with Cu2O was able to retard the recombination of the photogenerated charge carriers where CQDs serve as an electron acceptor to extract electrons from Cu2O. This behaviour is supported by the decrease in charge recombination rate of the CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite though PL analysis. The CQDs/Cu2O nanocomposite also exhibited an excellent photostability under repeated cycles of photoreaction. The considerable enhancement in the photoactivity of the nanocomposite describes the importance of the CQDs to facilitate the high electron demanding CO2 photoreduction reaction to C2H6.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank Ministry of Higher Education (MOHE), Malaysia for funding this research work under Fundamental Research Grant Scheme (FRGS/1/2015/TK10/UPM/02/6).
